---
title: "MESA — Mapping Epidemiology in Sparse Areas"
subtitle: "A desktop tool for public-health spatial analysis where data is thin"
---

::: {.tags .project-tags}
[Spatial statistics]{.tag} [C++]{.tag} [Desktop software]{.tag} [Geocoding]{.tag} [Public health]{.tag}
:::

## Problem

Most spatial-epidemiology tooling assumes you have a lot of data and a GIS
analyst to drive it. Rural and frontier health jurisdictions often have neither:
few cases, large and sparsely populated areas, and staff who need an answer
without writing code. Standard hot-spot methods can behave badly under those
conditions — small counts, irregular geography — and the good implementations
live in libraries that assume a Python or R workflow.

MESA is an attempt to put credible spatial-cluster analysis in front of people
who work in sparse-area public health, as a desktop application they can run
themselves.

## Approach

Build a self-contained C++ desktop app that takes addresses or points, geocodes
them, and runs local spatial-statistics to find clusters — with the statistics
implemented carefully and checked against reference libraries, so the output is
trustworthy rather than just plausible-looking. The emphasis is on being correct
and runnable offline, not on being a general-purpose GIS.

## Methods and tools

- **Geocoding.** Turn address or place data into coordinates for analysis.
- **Spatial cluster detection.** Local indicators of spatial association —
  Getis-Ord Gi\* for hot- and cold-spot detection and Moran's I for spatial
  autocorrelation.
- **Significance testing.** Monte Carlo / permutation testing for the local
  statistics, so significance reflects the data rather than an asymptotic
  assumption that may not hold at small counts.
- **Validation.** Statistical output is validated against established
  implementations — PySAL and R spatial packages — so results line up with
  known-good tools before the app is trusted.
- **Stack.** C++ desktop application, built to run locally without a server or
  cloud dependency.

## Status

Active personal project. The core spatial statistics and validation are the
part I care most about getting right; the application around them is the
delivery mechanism.

## What this demonstrates

- Implementing spatial statistics (Gi\*, Moran's I, permutation inference) from
  the ground up and validating them against reference libraries.
- Building performant, self-contained desktop software in C++.
- Designing a public-health tool for a specific, underserved use case rather
  than a generic one.

*Repository and build/download links will be added here.*
